name: Lyft Surge Pricing
overview:
Surge pricing dynamically increases ride prices in areas where demand exceeds supply. The system must
continuously monitor supply (available drivers) and demand (ride requests) per geographic zone, compute
surge multipliers in near-real-time, display surge zones to riders before they request a ride, and ensure pricing
is consistent throughout a ride (price locked at request time). The system must be fair, transparent, and
resistant to gaming.

inScope: Compute surge multiplier per geographic zone every 60 seconds
Display surge zones on rider map before requesting
Lock price at ride request time (no mid-ride price changes)

items: Support 500k active drivers and 2M riders across all surge zones
Surge multipliers recomputed every 60 seconds with <5 second end-to-end latency
Price locked at ride request time — multiplier must never change mid-ride
99.9% availability; stale surge data (up to 120s TTL) is preferable to unavailability
Surge zone updates pushed to riders in real-time via WebSocket on multiplier change
Surge history logged for regulatory audits and gaming detection

entities.name: SurgeZone
entities.schema:
{
  zone_id: string,  // geohash cell
  geohash: string,
  multiplier: float,  // 1.0 to 5.0

  demand_count: int,  // ride requests in last 5 min
  supply_count: int,  // available drivers
  computed_at: timestamp
}

approaches.title: Fixed Surge Thresholds
approaches.description: Apply surge when demand/supply ratio exceeds fixed thresholds (e.g., >2x demand = 1.5x surge). Simple but doesn't adapt to local conditions. A 2:1 ratio in a dense city is different from a rural area.
approaches.tradeoff: Not adaptive; same thresholds for all zones regardless of baseline conditions.

approaches.title: Demand/Supply Ratio
approaches.description: Surge multiplier = f(demand_count / supply_count) where f is a piecewise linear function. More adaptive than fixed thresholds. But raw counts don't account for driver velocity (a driver 5 minutes away is less valuable than one 1 minute away).
approaches.tradeoff: Doesn't account for driver proximity or velocity; can be gamed by drivers clustering.

approaches.title: ML-Based Surge with Supply/Demand Features
approaches.description: A gradient boosted model predicts the optimal surge multiplier using features: demand_count, supply_count, historical_demand (same time last week), weather, events, time_of_day. The model is trained to maximize driver utilization while minimizing rider wait time. Surge multipliers are computed every 60 seconds per geohash cell (precision 5 = ~5km cells) and stored in Redis.
approaches.tradeoff: ML model adds complexity; requires retraining as demand patterns change; harder to explain

approaches.title: to regulators.
approaches.description: Surge Zone Display Polling Rider app polls GET /surge/zones every 30 seconds. Surge zones change every 60 seconds; 30-second polling is acceptable but wastes bandwidth for riders not actively looking at the map.
approaches.tradeoff: Unnecessary bandwidth; slight delay in showing surge changes.

approaches.title: Push via WebSocket on Zone Change
approaches.description: When surge multipliers change, the Surge Service publishes zone updates to Kafka. A push service sends updates to riders in affected zones via WebSocket. Riders only receive updates when surge changes in their area. Reduces bandwidth and provides real-time surge visibility. Rider location is used to determine which zone updates to send.
approaches.tradeoff: Requires knowing rider location; WebSocket connection management overhead.

components.name: Service
components.responsibility: Reads demand/supply counts from Redis; runs ML model; writes surge multipliers to Redis

components.name: Demand Counter
components.responsibility: Counts ride requests per geohash cell in 5-minute sliding windows; stored in Redis

components.name: Supply Counter
components.responsibility: Counts available drivers per geohash cell; updated by Driver Location

components.name: Redis Surge Store
components.responsibility: Stores current surge multipliers per geohash cell; TTL = 120 seconds

components.name: Surge Push Service
components.responsibility: Detects multiplier changes; pushes zone updates to riders via WebSocket

components.name: Price Lock Service
components.responsibility: Locks the surge multiplier at ride request time; stores in Ride DB

questions.title: 2. Price Locking
questions.content:
When a rider requests a ride, the current surge multiplier for their pickup zone is locked into the ride record.
This multiplier is used for billing regardless of how surge changes during the ride. The Price Lock Service reads
the multiplier from Redis at request time and writes it to the Ride DB. This prevents the unfair scenario where a
rider's price increases mid-ride.

questions.title: 3. Surge Gaming Prevention
questions.content:
Drivers can game surge pricing by clustering in a zone to artificially reduce supply. The system detects this by
comparing the number of drivers in a zone against the number of completed rides from that zone. If the ratio is
abnormally high (many drivers, few rides), the supply count is adjusted downward. Additionally, surge
multipliers are smoothed over time to prevent rapid oscillations.

questions.title: 4. Regulatory Compliance
questions.content:
Some cities cap surge multipliers during emergencies (e.g., 1.5x during natural disasters). The Surge
Computation Service reads city-specific caps from a configuration store and applies them before writing to
Redis. Surge history is logged to Cassandra for regulatory audits.

questions.title: 5. Surge Prediction
questions.content:
A separate ML model predicts surge zones 15 minutes in advance using historical patterns, scheduled events
(concerts, sports games), and weather forecasts. Predicted surge zones are shown to drivers as 'heat maps' to
encourage them to pre-position in high-demand areas. This proactive supply management reduces the
frequency and magnitude of surge events.

keyFlows: ① Demand Counter tracks ride requests per geohash (5-min sliding window)
② Supply Counter tracks available drivers per geohash (from Redis Geo)
③ Surge Comp Svc runs ML model every 60s → writes multipliers to Redis
④ Changed multipliers → Kafka → Push Svc → WebSocket to riders in zone
⑤ Rider requests ride → Price Lock Svc reads Redis → locks multiplier in DB

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study dynamic pricing theory: how Lyft and Uber use surge pricing to balance supply and
demand, the elasticity of demand for ride-sharing, and the welfare effects of surge pricing.
Expert Note: Learn about geohash precision levels for surge zones: why precision 5 (~5km cells) is
appropriate for surge, and how to aggregate counts across cells for smooth zone boundaries.
Expert Note: Understand sliding window counters in Redis: how to implement a 5-minute sliding window
using sorted sets (ZADD with timestamp as score, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to expire old entries).
Expert Note: Study gradient boosted trees for pricing models: how to handle temporal features
(time-of-day, day-of-week), how to prevent overfitting to historical surge patterns.
Expert Note: Learn about price locking in distributed systems: how to ensure the locked price is consistent
across the ride request, matching, and billing services using a distributed transaction or saga pattern.
